8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

*AI can turn a prompt into a working app in an afternoon. This checklist helps you keep that speed without shipping exposed secrets, broken access control, or an app nobody can safely maintain.*
By Termi Protocol
The dangerous part of vibe coding is not that AI writes bad code every time.
It is that the result can look finished before anyone has checked the parts users cannot see.
The page loads. The login form accepts an email. The dashboard displays data. The demo feels convincing. Meanwhile, a secret may be sitting in the client bundle, a database rule may trust the browser, or an admin route may only be hidden by the interface.
That gap between “works in the demo” and “safe enough to ship” is where vibe coding security lives.
This is not a replacement for a professional security review. It is a practical release gate for prototypes, internal tools, and small apps built with AI. If the product handles money, health data, identity documents, children’s data, or other high-impact decisions, treat this checklist as the beginning — not the approval.
## First, classify what you are building
Security effort should match the possible harm.
Before asking an AI agent to write more code, put the project in one of these three buckets:
| Level | Example | Minimum release decision |
| — -| — -| — -|
| Low stakes | Local calculator, throwaway prototype, personal script | Keep it local; do not add real user data |
| Moderate stakes | Public landing page, waitlist, small internal tool | Complete this checklist and review every external service |
| High stakes | Payments, medical data, financial records, production authentication | Require experienced human review and dedicated security testing |
The most useful security improvement is often removing a feature. A prototype that does not collect personal data cannot leak that personal data. A demo that uses fake records does not need production database access.
Minimize the blast radius before you harden the code.
## 1. Create a simple threat model
A threat model does not need to be a forty-page document. Answer five questions in plain language:
1. What valuable data or action does this app control?
2. Who should be allowed to access it?
3. What could an anonymous visitor try to do?
4. Which external services receive data?
5. What is the worst believable failure?
For a small appointment app, the valuable assets may be customer names, contact details, staff schedules, and the ability to cancel a booking. The worst failure may be one customer reading another customer’s appointment or an anonymous visitor deleting the calendar.
Give this model to the coding agent. Ask it to map each risk to the route, database rule, or integration that controls it. Do not accept “the framework handles security” as an answer.
## 2. Remove secrets from prompts, code, and screenshots
API keys, access tokens, database passwords, private certificates, and service-account files do not belong in source code or chat messages.
If a real secret has been pasted into a prompt, committed to Git, shown in a public screenshot, or added to client-side code, treat it as exposed. Removing the text is not enough. Revoke or rotate the credential, then replace it through the platform’s secret manager or a local environment variable.
Ask the agent to search for common secret patterns, but also inspect the result yourself. Generated code may place a server credential in a `.env` file and then accidentally expose that variable through a browser build prefix.
Your release gate should include:
- No real credentials in the repository history
- No secrets in frontend JavaScript bundles
- No production keys in examples or test fixtures
- A committed `.env.example` containing names only, never values
- Different credentials for local development and production
## 3. Enforce authorization on the server
Authentication asks, “Who are you?” Authorization asks, “Are you allowed to do this?”
A common AI-generated shortcut is to hide an admin button in the interface and assume the route behind it is protected. Another is to send a user ID from the browser and trust it when reading database rows.
Neither is authorization.
Every sensitive read, write, update, and delete must be checked in a trusted server or database policy. Change the URL manually. Change the record ID. Call the endpoint without using the interface. Try a second account.
For each protected action, test at least these states:
- Signed out
- Signed in as the correct user
- Signed in as a different user
- Signed in as a normal user attempting an admin action
A button being invisible is a design detail. A server rejecting the request is security.
## 4. Validate input where it becomes trusted
Browser validation improves usability, but it is easy to bypass.
Validate input again on the server. Define accepted types, lengths, formats, and ranges. Reject unexpected fields instead of silently storing them. Use allowlists when the valid options are known.
Pay special attention to inputs that become:
- Database queries
- File paths or filenames
- Shell commands
- URLs fetched by the server
- HTML displayed to another user
- Redirect destinations
Do not ask the model to “sanitize everything” and move on. Ask which exact boundary validates each input and what test proves it.
## 5. Keep database access narrow
Convenient development permissions often survive into production.
Review database roles, row-level policies, service credentials, and public access. The application should receive only the permissions required for the current action. A public form may need permission to create one limited record; it does not need permission to list, update, and delete the entire table.
Seed development with fake data. Use a separate production project or database. Back up important records and test that you can restore them before a migration needs it.
If the agent proposes disabling a policy “temporarily” to make the demo work, record that as a release blocker.
## 6. Review every dependency the agent adds
AI coding agents are very good at solving a small problem by adding a package.
Every dependency increases the code you trust, the licenses you must respect, and the updates you may need to manage. Before accepting a new package, ask:
- Is it actively maintained?
- Is the package name correct, or is it a look-alike?
- Can the platform or standard library already do this?
- What version was installed?
- Does the lockfile record the exact dependency tree?
- Does the install script run code on your machine?
Run the ecosystem’s dependency audit, but do not treat “zero known vulnerabilities” as proof that the package is safe. It only means the scanner did not match a known advisory.
## 7. Put dangerous actions behind explicit gates
Some commands deserve more friction than others.
Reading a file and running a unit test are different from deleting a directory, applying a production migration, changing cloud permissions, or deploying to customers. Configure approval rules so the agent cannot cross those boundaries silently.
Human approval should normally be required for:
- Destructive file or database operations
- Production deployments
- Infrastructure and permission changes
- Credential creation or rotation
- Billing changes
- Sending messages or modifying third-party systems
The exact list depends on the project. The principle does not: convenience should not erase accountability.
## 8. Make changes reversible
Vibe coding becomes safer when every experiment has an exit.
Use Git from the first meaningful version. Start a small branch for a risky feature. Commit before a migration. Keep generated changes narrow enough to review. Save a working checkpoint before asking for a broad refactor.
“The agent can fix it” is not a rollback strategy.
For data changes, reversibility may require a backup, a down migration, or a forward repair plan. Test the recovery path before production rather than discovering during an incident that the backup is incomplete.
## 9. Test abuse cases, not only the happy path
Generated tests often prove that generated code behaves as the model expected. Add adversarial cases that challenge those expectations.
Try:
- Empty, oversized, malformed, and duplicated input
- Direct requests that skip the interface
- Another user’s record ID
- Repeated password or verification attempts
- An expired or missing session
- Uploads with unexpected types and filenames
- Network failure halfway through a multi-step action
- Refreshing or submitting the same payment-like action twice
Ask the AI agent to propose abuse cases, then choose and run them yourself. A test written but never executed is documentation, not evidence.
## 10. Add logs without logging sensitive data
When something goes wrong, you need enough evidence to understand the event.
Log authentication failures, permission denials, server errors, administrative actions, and important state transitions. Include a request or correlation ID so one action can be followed across services.
Do not log passwords, session tokens, full payment data, private keys, or entire request bodies by default. Logs are another copy of your data and need access control, retention limits, and deletion rules.
Before release, trigger a controlled failure and confirm that the log helps you diagnose it without exposing the user.
## 11. Separate preview from production
A preview is a place to learn. Production is a promise to users.
Use different credentials, databases, domains, and analytics settings where possible. Confirm that debug mode is off, error pages do not reveal internals, test accounts are removed, and development-only routes are unavailable.
Create a release checklist that names the exact environment. “Deploy” is too ambiguous when the same agent can reach local, preview, staging, and production systems.
## 12. Write an incident and ownership note
Before launch, answer:
- Who can take the app offline?
- Who can rotate each credential?
- Where are backups and logs?
- How will affected users be contacted?
- Which person owns dependency and platform updates?
Even a one-person project needs these answers. Future you is still an operator who may be tired, traveling, or returning to the code months later.
Also write down which parts were AI-generated and which risky areas received human review. This makes the next audit faster and keeps confidence proportional to evidence.
## A 20-minute pre-release gate
If time is short, do not pretend all twelve sections received a full review. Run this smaller gate and keep the product private if any answer is unclear:
1. Rotate any credential that entered chat, Git, or frontend code.
2. Test one protected record with a second user account.
3. Call one sensitive endpoint without the interface.
4. Review newly added dependencies and the lockfile.
5. Run tests and the dependency/security scanner.
6. Confirm the last working version can be restored.
7. Verify that logs capture a failure without recording secrets.
8. Name the human who approves production deployment.
This gate will not make an application secure by itself. It can stop the most preventable mistake: shipping because the interface looked complete.
## Safe vibe coding is supervised autonomy
The goal is not to watch every token or manually rewrite every generated line.
The goal is to place strong controls at the boundaries where mistakes become expensive: credentials, permissions, data, dependencies, destructive commands, and production releases.
That is also why visibility matters. A transcript is useful, but a developer needs a quick answer to concrete questions: what the agent is trying to do, which files changed, which command is waiting for approval, and which checkpoint can restore the project.
We built Termi Protocol around that problem. It is a visual control layer for coding agents, not a security scanner and not a replacement for review
